is a main [federal road](/wiki/Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System "Malaysian Federal Roads System") running along the east coast of Peninsula [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). The 739 kilometres (459 mi) federal highway connects [Rantau Panjang](/wiki/Rantau_Panjang "Rantau Panjang") (near the border with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")) in [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan") until [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") in [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor").[[3]](#cite_note-jkr-3) The entire FT3 highway is gazetted as a part of the [Asian Highway Network](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network") route 18.[[4]](#cite_note-asian-highway-4)

first_paragraph: **Federal Route 3** is a main [federal road](/wiki/Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System "Malaysian Federal Roads System") running along the east coast of Peninsula [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). The 739 kilometres (459 mi) federal highway connects [Rantau Panjang](/wiki/Rantau_Panjang "Rantau Panjang") (near the border with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")) in [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan") until [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") in [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor").[[3]](#cite_note-jkr-3) The entire FT3 highway is gazetted as a part of the [Asian Highway Network](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network") route 18.[[4]](#cite_note-asian-highway-4)

**Federal Route 3** is a main [federal road](/wiki/Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System "Malaysian Federal Roads System") running along the east coast of Peninsula [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). The 739 kilometres (459 mi) federal highway connects [Rantau Panjang](/wiki/Rantau_Panjang "Rantau Panjang") (near the border with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand")) in [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan") until [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") in [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor").[[3]](#cite_note-jkr-3) The entire FT3 highway is gazetted as a part of the [Asian Highway Network](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network") route 18.[[4]](#cite_note-asian-highway-4)

The Federal Route 3 has gained a reputation as one of the best coastal highways in Malaysia and Asia due to the scenic views along the highway, as being recognised by the [National Geographic Society](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") and [Harian Metro](/wiki/Harian_Metro "Harian Metro"). The Federal Route 3 is named as one of the top 10 coastal highways in Asia by the National Geographic Society,[[5]](#cite_note-natgeo-5) while Harian Metro recognises the FT3 highway as one of the best highways in Malaysia.[[6]](#cite_note-hmetro-6)

## Route background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysia_Federal_Route_3&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Terengganusign.png/250px-Terengganusign.png)](/wiki/File:Terengganusign.png)

A direction signboard on the northbound lane of Federal Route 3 in [Terengganu](/wiki/Terengganu "Terengganu").

The Federal Route 3 is one of the three north–south backbone federal highways in Peninsular Malaysia; the other two are the [Federal Routes 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1") and [5](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_5 "Malaysia Federal Route 5").[[7]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya2-7) Generally, the Federal Route 3 runs mostly along the east coast of Peninsular Malaysia.[[8]](#cite_note-jkr-inventori-8)

The FT3 highway begins as a [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") at Johor Bahru Interchange that links with the Federal Route 1, the main trunk road of the central Peninsular Malaysia, at its first kilometer. Then at [Kota Tinggi](/wiki/Kota_Tinggi "Kota Tinggi"), the FT3 becomes a super two highway until [Pekan](/wiki/Pekan_District "Pekan District"). However, the FT3 is not a coastal highway yet; it is only at Mersing that the FT3 highway starts to become a coastal highway. At Pekan, the FT3 crosses the [Pahang River](/wiki/Pahang_River "Pahang River") via the [Sultan Abu Bakar Bridge](/wiki/Abu_Bakar_Bridge "Abu Bakar Bridge") FT3 and proceeds to [Kuantan](/wiki/Kuantan "Kuantan") as a divided highway.[[8]](#cite_note-jkr-inventori-8)

At Kuantan, the FT3 concurrents with [Gambang–Kuantan Highway](/wiki/Gambang%E2%80%93Kuantan_Highway "Gambang–Kuantan Highway") FT2 briefly from Exit 254 Jalan Pekan Interchange to Exit 253 Pandan Interchange before the FT3 is diverted to [Kuantan Bypass](/wiki/Kuantan_Bypass "Kuantan Bypass") FT3.[[9]](#cite_note-:02-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12) The Kuantan Bypass FT3 continues as the Kuantan–Kuala Terengganu Road FT3 that runs along the coastline of Terengganu. At [Kuala Terengganu](/wiki/Kuala_Terengganu "Kuala Terengganu"), the FT3 highway is diverted to Kuala Terengganu–Kota Bharu Road FT3 that runs along the northern interior region of Terengganu and the plains of northern Kelantan. At Kota Bharu, the FT3 crosses the [Kelantan River](/wiki/Kelantan_River "Kelantan River") via the [Sultan Yahya Petra Bridge](/wiki/Sultan_Yahya_Petra_Bridge "Sultan Yahya Petra Bridge") FT3 to [Wakaf Bharu](/wiki/Wakaf_Bharu "Wakaf Bharu") before running along the west bank of the Kelantan River to [Pasir Mas](/wiki/Pasir_Mas "Pasir Mas"). At Pasir Mas, the FT3 highway is diverted west towards [Rantau Panjang](/wiki/Rantau_Panjang "Rantau Panjang") at the Malaysia–Thailand border. At the Malaysia–Thailand border, the FT3 highway crosses the [Golok River](/wiki/Golok_River "Golok River") via the [Rantau Panjang–Sungai Golok Bridge](/wiki/Rantau_Panjang%E2%80%93Sungai_Golok_Bridge "Rantau Panjang–Sungai Golok Bridge") and continues as [Sungai Padi Road](/wiki/Sungai_Padi_Road "Sungai Padi Road") (Route 4056) and [Route 42](/wiki/Highway_42_(Thailand) "Highway 42 (Thailand)") in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[8]](#cite_note-jkr-inventori-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysia_Federal_Route_3&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Malaysia_Federal_Highway_3_%28Jalan_Kelantan%29%2C_Kampung_Durian_Burung_20240226_211253.jpg/250px-Malaysia_Federal_Highway_3_%28Jalan_Kelantan%29%2C_Kampung_Durian_Burung_20240226_211253.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysia_Federal_Highway_3_(Jalan_Kelantan),_Kampung_Durian_Burung_20240226_211253.jpg)

Federal Route 3 at Kuala Terengganu at night

The Federal Route 3 began as an 8 miles (13 km) short road from [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") to Sungai Pandan, which formed the present-day [Tebrau Highway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru%E2%80%93Kota_Tinggi_Highway "Johor Bahru–Kota Tinggi Highway") FT3. It was constructed in 1866 by Dato' [Muhamad Salleh bin Perang](/wiki/Muhamad_Salleh_bin_Perang "Muhamad Salleh bin Perang") (also known as Dato' Bentara Luar).[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1) The road was later extended to [Kota Tinggi](/wiki/Kota_Tinggi "Kota Tinggi"), which was completed in 1919.[[13]](#cite_note-uitm1-13) Meanwhile, in 1915, the Kuala Terengganu–Kota Bharu Road FT3 was constructed, which was completed in 1923.[[14]](#cite_note-jkr2-14)[[15]](#cite_note-uitm2-15)

In 1911, the state government of [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor") collaborated with the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") colonial government to develop a road network from [Johor Bahru](/wiki/Johor_Bahru "Johor Bahru") to [Batu Pahat](/wiki/Batu_Pahat "Batu Pahat") and Muar.[[16]](#cite_note-16) As a result, the [Batu Pahat–Kluang–Mersing Road](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_50 "Malaysia Federal Route 50") was completed in 1919,[[13]](#cite_note-uitm1-13) where the section from Jemaluang to Mersing formed a part of the present-day Federal Route 3.[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1) At the same time, another section of road between Kota Tinggi to Jemaluang was constructed to link between both Johor Bahru–Kota Tinggi Road FT3 and Jemaluang–Mersing FT3.[[13]](#cite_note-uitm1-13) In 1939, the Kuantan–Kuala Terengganu Road FT3 and the Kuantan–Pekan Road FT3 (up until Peramu at the north bank of the [Pahang River](/wiki/Pahang_River "Pahang River")) was constructed.[[15]](#cite_note-uitm2-15)[[17]](#cite_note-uitm3-17)

Unlike major roads in western states of Malaya, the construction progress of the Federal Route 3 was sluggish, due to the lack of economic resources in the eastern states of Malaya. As a result, a gap between [Pekan](/wiki/Pekan_District "Pekan District") to [Endau](/wiki/Endau "Endau") existed due to very scarce population and swampy terrain.[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1) To fill in the gap, the first post-independent [Malaysian Minister of Works](/wiki/Malaysian_Ministry_of_Works "Malaysian Ministry of Works"), Tun [V.T. Sambanthan](/wiki/V.T._Sambanthan "V.T. Sambanthan"), requested the federal government to allocate a large amount of budget to fund the construction of newer rural roads, as well as the rehabilitation of existing roads. The construction of newer rural road network grew from 400 miles (644 km) in 1961 to 700 miles (1,127 km) in the next year. Among these, the longest road projects were the Pekan–Kuala Rompin Road FT3 and Endau–Kuala Rompin Road FT3, both completed in 1962.[[2]](#cite_note-um-2) The completion of both roads had thus concluded the construction of the entire Federal Route 3.[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1)

Like [its west coastal counterpart](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_5 "Malaysia Federal Route 5"), the construction of the Federal Route 3 required a lot of longer bridges. As a result, some major bridges such as the [Sultan Yahya Petra Bridge](/wiki/Sultan_Yahya_Petra_Bridge "Sultan Yahya Petra Bridge") FT3 in [Kota Bharu](/wiki/Kota_Bharu "Kota Bharu") and the [Sultan Abu Bakar Bridge](/wiki/Abu_Bakar_Bridge "Abu Bakar Bridge") in Pekan were constructed as toll bridges to help recovering the construction costs. The Sultan Yahya Petra Bridge FT3 was built in 1962 and was opened on 17 April 1965,[[18]](#cite_note-hids1-18) while the Sultan Abu Bakar Bridge FT3 was built in 1968 and was opened on 28 February 1970.[[19]](#cite_note-hids2-19) Meanwhile, another major bridge along the FT3, the Pulau Rusa Bridge FT3 near [Kuala Terengganu](/wiki/Kuala_Terengganu "Kuala Terengganu"), was constructed in 1960.[[20]](#cite_note-hmetro2-20)

On 1 September 1974, the Kuantan Port Authority was established to build the [Kuantan Port](/wiki/Kuantan_Port "Kuantan Port"). The construction began in 1976 and was fully opened in 1984.[[21]](#cite_note-kuantan-port-21) As a result, [a new roadway bypassing Kuantan](/wiki/Kuantan_Bypass "Kuantan Bypass") was built in order to reduce the road congestion in [Kuantan](/wiki/Kuantan "Kuantan") as well as to minimize the road damage caused by heavy vehicles commuting to Kuantan Port.[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1) The Kuantan Bypass FT3 was later being upgraded into a [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") in 2005, which was completed in 2007.[[22]](#cite_note-rmk9-22)

The entire section of the FT3 highway forms the Malaysian section of the [Asian Highway](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network") Route [AH18](/wiki/AH18 "AH18").[[4]](#cite_note-asian-highway-4) As a result, sections of the Federal Route 3 are progressively being upgraded to either [super two](/wiki/Super_two "Super two") highways or [divided highways](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway").[[1]](#cite_note-blogjalanraya1-1) Besides, the Federal Route 3 remains popular as a scenic coastal route of the east coast of Peninsular Malaysia and is unaffected by the presence of the [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway") Phase 2 (ECE2) E8, due to the fact that the FT3 highway has been recognized by the [National Geographic Society](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") as one of the top 10 coastal highways in Asia.[[5]](#cite_note-natgeo-5)

The Federal Route 3 that pass through Kijal and Teluk Kalong is gazetted as Federal Route 145, the original Federal Route 145 ([Kemaman Bypass](/wiki/Kemaman_Bypass "Kemaman Bypass")) is gazetted as part of Federal Route 3.

**Kuantan Bypass**, **Federal Route 3, AH18**, often called as Bypass Kuantan or Jalan Pintasan Kuantan is a main highway bypass in [Kuantan](/wiki/Kuantan "Kuantan"), [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[[9]](#cite_note-:02-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:1-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:2-12)

### Route background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysia_Federal_Route_3&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Route background")]

The Kuantan Bypass starts at Kuantan Airport South Interchange, at its interchange with the [Federal Route 2](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_2 "Malaysia Federal Route 2"), the main trunk road of the central of Peninsular Malaysia.

At most sections, the Kuantan Bypass was built under the JKR R5 road standard, allowing maximum speed limit of up to 90 km/h.

There are no alternate routes or sections with motorcycle lanes.

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysia_Federal_Route_3&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: History")]

It was built in 1977 until it was completed in 1979.

The upgrade from trunk road bypass into dual carriageway bypass was begun in 2005 and was completed in 2007.

On 6 August 2025, Twin Box Culvert construction at Kuantan Bypass started and completed on 5 September 2025.[[9]](#cite_note-:02-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)
